# Monkey-patch upsetplot to fix nan edgecolor bug with matplotlib 3.10+
import upsetplot.plotting as up_plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

_orig_plot_matrix = up_plt.UpSet.plot_matrix

def _patched_plot_matrix(self, ax):
    ax = self._reorient(ax)
    data = self.intersections
    n_cats = data.index.nlevels
    inclusion = data.index.to_frame().values

    styles = [
        [
            self.subset_styles[i]
            if inclusion[i, j]
            else {'facecolor': self._other_dots_color, 'linewidth': 0}
            for j in range(n_cats)
        ]
        for i in range(len(data))
    ]
    styles = sum(styles, [])
    style_columns = {
        'facecolor': 'facecolors',
        'edgecolor': 'edgecolors',
        'linewidth': 'linewidths',
        'linestyle': 'linestyles',
        'hatch': 'hatch',
    }
    styles_df = (
        pd.DataFrame(styles)
        .reindex(columns=style_columns.keys())
        .astype({'facecolor': 'O', 'edgecolor': 'O', 'linewidth': float, 'linestyle': 'O', 'hatch': 'O'})
    )
    styles_df['linewidth'] = styles_df['linewidth'].fillna(1)
    styles_df['facecolor'] = styles_df['facecolor'].fillna(self._facecolor)
    styles_df['edgecolor'] = styles_df['edgecolor'].fillna(styles_df['facecolor'])
    # Replace any remaining nan with facecolor (to handle Copy-on-Write issue)
    mask = styles_df['edgecolor'].isna()
    styles_df.loc[mask, 'edgecolor'] = styles_df.loc[mask, 'facecolor']
    styles_df['linestyle'] = styles_df['linestyle'].fillna('solid')
    del styles_df['hatch']

    x = np.repeat(np.arange(len(data)), n_cats)
    y = np.tile(np.arange(n_cats), len(data))

    if self._element_size is not None:
        s = (self._element_size * 0.35) ** 2
    else:
        s = 200
    ax.scatter(
        *self._swapaxes(x, y),
        s=s,
        zorder=10,
        **styles_df.rename(columns=style_columns),
    )

    if self._with_lines:
        idx = np.flatnonzero(inclusion)
        line_data = (
            pd.Series(y[idx], index=x[idx])
            .groupby(level=0)
            .aggregate(['min', 'max'])
        )
        colors = pd.Series(
            [style.get('edgecolor', style.get('facecolor', self._facecolor)) for style in self.subset_styles],
            name='color',
        )
        line_data = line_data.join(colors)
        ax.vlines(line_data.index.values, line_data['min'], line_data['max'], lw=2, colors=line_data['color'], zorder=5)

    tick_axis = ax.yaxis
    tick_axis.set_ticks(np.arange(n_cats))
    tick_axis.set_ticklabels(data.index.names, rotation=0 if self._horizontal else -90)
    ax.xaxis.set_visible(False)
    ax.tick_params(axis='both', which='both', length=0)
    if not self._horizontal:
        ax.yaxis.set_ticks_position('top')
    ax.set_frame_on(False)
    ax.set_xlim(-0.5, x[-1] + 0.5, auto=False)
    ax.grid(False)

up_plt.UpSet.plot_matrix = _patched_plot_matrix
print('Monkey-patch applied')

# Now test
from upsetplot import UpSet, from_contents
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

avg_positions = set(top20_by_avg['position'].tolist())
top_positions = set(top20_by_top['position'].tolist())
contents = {'Top-20 by average': avg_positions, 'Top-20 by peak': top_positions}
upset_data2 = from_contents(contents)

fig2 = plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
UpSet(upset_data2, subset_size='count', show_counts=True).plot(fig=fig2)
print('UpSet plot success!')
plt.close(fig2)

@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(df_spm, plt, sns):
    df_heatmap = df_spm.pivot_table(
        index="mutant_aa", 
        columns="position", 
        values="mean", 
        aggfunc="mean"
    )

    plt.figure(figsize=(24, 10))
    sns.heatmap(
        df_heatmap, 
        cmap="viridis", 
        cbar_kws={"label": "Mean Value"},
        xticklabels=50
    )
    plt.xlabel("Position")
    plt.ylabel("Mutant Amino Acid")
    plt.title("Heatmap of Mean Values by Position and Mutant Amino Acid")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(df_spm):
    pos_avg = df_spm.groupby("position")["mean"].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
    pos_top = df_spm.groupby("position")["mean"].max().sort_values(ascending=False)

    print("Top 20 positions by average mean value:")
    print(pos_avg.head(20))

    print("Top 20 positions by top mean value:")
    print(pos_top.head(20))

@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(pos_avg, pos_top):

    top20_avg = set(pos_avg.head(20).index)
    top20_top = set(pos_top.head(20).index)

    print(f"Top 20 by avg: {sorted(top20_avg)}")
    print(f"Top 20 by top: {sorted(top20_top)}")
    print(f"Overlap: {sorted(top20_avg & top20_top)}")
    print(f"Only in avg: {sorted(top20_avg - top20_top)}")
    print(f"Only in top: {sorted(top20_top - top20_avg)}")
    return top20_avg, top20_top


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(plt, pos_avg, pos_top):
    from upsetplot import UpSet, from_memberships

    top20_avg_list = list(pos_avg.head(20).index)
    top20_top_list = list(pos_top.head(20).index)

    memberships = [
        ["Top20_by_Avg", "Top20_by_Max"],
    ]

    data = []
    for pos in top20_avg_list:
        cats = ["Top20_by_Avg"]
        if pos in top20_top_list:
            cats.append("Top20_by_Max")
        data.append(cats)

    upset_data = from_memberships(data)
    fig = plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
    upset = UpSet(upset_data, subset_size="count", show_counts=True, sort_by="cardinality")
    upset.plot(fig=fig)
    plt.suptitle("UpSet Plot: Overlap of Top 20 Positions (Avg vs Max)")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)

if __name__ == "__main__":

import marimo

app = marimo.App()


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(mo):
    mo.md(r"""
    ## Imports

    We import the core libraries needed for data loading, visualization, and set analysis.
    """)
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _():
    import marimo as mo
    import pandas as pd
    import numpy as np
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    import seaborn as sns
    from pathlib import Path
    from upsetplot import UpSet

    return Path, mo, pd, plt, sns


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(mo):
    mo.md(r"""
    ## Load the Dataset

    We read the IRED Novartis mutation screening CSV into a pandas DataFrame. This dataset contains mutation labels, activity means, and other assay metrics for an imine reductase (IRED) enzyme engineering campaign.
    """)
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(Path, pd):
    data_path = Path('data/ired-novartis/cs1c02786_si_002.csv')
    df_raw = pd.read_csv(data_path, index_col=0)
    df_raw.head(10)
    return (df_raw,)


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(mo):
    mo.md(r"""
    ## Identify Single Point Mutations

    The mutation column contains both single and multi-point mutations. Multi-point mutations are separated by semicolons (e.g. "A111T; S220T"). We filter to only single-point mutations, then parse out the wildtype residue, numeric position, and mutant residue from labels like "A111C" (Ala at position 111 mutated to Cys).
    """)
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(df_raw):
    # Filter to single-point mutations (no semicolons in the mutation string)
    # Also drop any rows with missing mutation values
    df_single = df_raw[~df_raw['mutation'].str.contains(';', na=True)].copy()
    df_single = df_single.dropna(subset=['mutation'])

    # Parse mutation components: wildtype letter, position, mutant letter
    df_single['wildtype'] = df_single['mutation'].str[0]
    df_single['position'] = df_single['mutation'].str.extract(r'(\d+)')[0].astype(int)
    df_single['mutant'] = df_single['mutation'].str[-1]

    print(f'Total rows: {len(df_raw):,}')
    print(f'Single-point mutations: {len(df_single):,}')
    print(f'Multi-point mutations: {len(df_raw) - len(df_single):,}')
    print(f'Unique positions: {df_single["position"].nunique()}')
    print(f'Unique mutant residues: {df_single["mutant"].nunique()}')
    df_single.head(10)
    return (df_single,)


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(mo):
    mo.md(r"""
    ## Heatmap of Single-Point Mutation Activity

    We pivot the single-point mutation data into a matrix with position on the x-axis and mutant amino acid on the y-axis, using the mean activity as the heatmap value. This gives us a comprehensive view of how each substitution at each position affects enzyme activity. Positions are sorted numerically and amino acids are sorted alphabetically.
    """)
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(df_single, plt, sns):
    # Pivot to create the heatmap matrix: position (x) vs mutant letter (y), value = mean
    heatmap_data = df_single.pivot_table(
        index='mutant',
        columns='position',
        values='mean',
        aggfunc='mean'
    )

    # Sort axes
    heatmap_data = heatmap_data.sort_index()  # alphabetical mutant letters
    heatmap_data = heatmap_data[sorted(heatmap_data.columns)]  # numeric positions

    fig_heatmap, ax_heatmap = plt.subplots(figsize=(40, 8))
    sns.heatmap(
        heatmap_data,
        cmap='YlOrRd',
        ax=ax_heatmap,
        xticklabels=True,
        yticklabels=True,
        cbar_kws={'label': 'Mean Activity'},
        linewidths=0,
    )
    ax_heatmap.set_xlabel('Position')
    ax_heatmap.set_ylabel('Mutant Amino Acid')
    ax_heatmap.set_title('Single-Point Mutation Activity Heatmap')
    ax_heatmap.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=90, labelsize=5)
    ax_heatmap.tick_params(axis='y', rotation=0, labelsize=8)
    plt.tight_layout()
    fig_heatmap
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(mo):
    mo.md(r"""
    ## Rank Positions by Activity

    We rank positions two ways to identify the most promising mutation sites. First, we rank by the average value of the mean column across all mutant residues at each position — this highlights positions that are broadly tolerant to or improved by substitution. Second, we rank by the top (maximum) value of the mean column at each position — this highlights positions where at least one substitution produced a strong effect. We take the top 20 from each ranking for comparison.
    """)
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(df_single):
    # Rank positions by average mean activity
    pos_avg_mean = df_single.groupby('position')['mean'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
    top20_avg = set(pos_avg_mean.head(20).index.tolist())

    # Rank positions by top (max) mean activity
    pos_max_mean = df_single.groupby('position')['mean'].max().sort_values(ascending=False)
    top20_max = set(pos_max_mean.head(20).index.tolist())

    print('Top 20 positions by AVERAGE mean:')
    print(pos_avg_mean.head(20).to_string())
    print()
    print('Top 20 positions by MAX mean:')
    print(pos_max_mean.head(20).to_string())
    print()
    print(f'Overlap: {len(top20_avg & top20_max)} positions appear in both top-20 lists')
    print(f'Positions in both: {sorted(top20_avg & top20_max)}')
    print(f'Only in avg top-20: {sorted(top20_avg - top20_max)}')
    print(f'Only in max top-20: {sorted(top20_max - top20_avg)}')
    return top20_avg, top20_max


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(mo):
    mo.md(r"""
    ## UpSet Plot: Overlap of Top 20 Positions

    The UpSet plot visualizes the set relationships between the two ranking methods. Each position in the union of both top-20 lists is assigned membership in one or both sets. The intersection bars show how many positions are shared between rankings versus unique to each, helping us distinguish positions that are consistently high-performing (in both lists) from those that are high only by one metric.
    """)
    return


@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(plt, top20_avg, top20_max):
    from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec

    # Define the three subsets
    only_avg = sorted(top20_avg - top20_max)
    only_max = sorted(top20_max - top20_avg)
    both_sets = sorted(top20_avg & top20_max)

    subset_labels = ['Top 20 by Average\nonly', 'Both', 'Top 20 by Max\nonly']
    subset_sizes = [len(only_avg), len(both_sets), len(only_max)]
    subset_membership = [
        (True, False),   # avg only
        (True, True),    # both
        (False, True),   # max only
    ]
    set_names = ['Top 20 by Average', 'Top 20 by Max']
    set_totals = [len(top20_avg), len(top20_max)]

    fig_upset = plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

@app.cell(hide_code=True)

@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(plt, top20_by_avg, top20_by_top):
    from upsetplot import UpSet, from_memberships

    avg_positions = set(top20_by_avg["position"].tolist())
    top_positions = set(top20_by_top["position"].tolist())

    """)

@app.cell(hide_code=True)
def _(df_raw, pd):
    import re

    mutation_pattern = re.compile(r'^([A-Z])(\d+)([A-Z])$')

    def parse_mutation(mutation_str):
        if not isinstance(mutation_str, str):
            return None, None, None
        match = mutation_pattern.match(mutation_str)
        if match:
            return match.group(1), int(match.group(2)), match.group(3)
        return None, None, None

    df_single = df_raw[~df_raw['mutation'].str.contains(';', na=False)].copy()
    df_single[['wildtype', 'position', 'mutant']] = df_single['mutation'].apply(
        lambda m: pd.Series(parse_mutation(m))
    )
    df_single = df_single.dropna(subset=['position'])
    df_single['position'] = df_single['position'].astype(int)
    df_single
    return (df_single,)

{"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1775620224503,"sessionID":"ses_294c9a442ffe2u1rupbrxwDiTe","part":{"id":"prt_d6b3675e3001Ra46P70L7cd0FN","messageID":"msg_d6b365df1001NVM3O1ZwSA0yH9","sessionID":"ses_294c9a442ffe2u1rupbrxwDiTe","type":"tool","tool":"skill","callID":"call_4300aa148748427e8a7db7c4","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"name":"marimo-pair"},"output":"<skill_content name=\"marimo-pair\">\n# Skill: marimo-pair\n\n# marimo Pair Programming Protocol\n\nThis skill gives you full access to a running marimo notebook. You can read\ncell code, create and edit cells, install packages, run cells, and inspect\nthe reactive graph — all programmatically. The user sees results live in their\nbrowser while you work through bundled scripts or MCP.\n\n## Philosophy\n\nmarimo notebooks are a dataflow graph — cells are the fundamental unit of\ncomputation, connected by the variables they define and reference. When a cell\nruns, marimo automatically re-executes downstream cells. You have full access\nto the running notebook.\n\n- **Cells are your main lever.** Use them to break up work and choose how and\n  when to bring the human into the loop. Not every cell needs rich output —\n  sometimes the object itself is enough, sometimes a summary is better.\n  Match the presentation to the intent.\n- **Understand intent first.** When clear, act. When ambiguous, clarify.\n- **Follow existing signal.** Check imports, `pyproject.toml`, existing cells,\n  and `dir(ctx)` before reaching for external tools.\n- **Stay focused.** Build first, polish later — cell names, layout, and styling\n  can wait.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n### How to invoke marimo\n\nOnly servers started with `--no-token` register in the local server registry\nand are auto-discoverable — starting without a token makes discovery easier.\nIf a server has a token, set the `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable before\ncalling the execute script (avoids leaking the token in process listings). The\nright way to invoke marimo depends on context (project\ntooling, global install, sandbox mode). See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md) for the full decision tree.\n\n**Do NOT use `--headless` unless the user asks for it.** Omitting it lets\nmarimo auto-open the browser, which is the expected pairing experience. If the\nuser explicitly requests headless, offer to open it with\n`open http://localhost:<port>`.\n\n## How to Discover Servers and Execute Code\n\nTwo operations: **discover servers** and **execute code**.\n\n| Operation | Script | MCP |\n|-----------|--------|-----|\n| Discover servers | `bash scripts/discover-servers.sh` | `list_sessions()` tool |\n| Execute code | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh -c \"code\"` | `execute_code(code=..., session_id=...)` tool |\n| Execute code (multiline) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'` | same |\n| Execute code (direct URL) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh --url URL -c \"code\"` | same (with `url` param) |\n\nScripts auto-discover sessions from the registry on disk. Use `--port` to\ntarget a specific server when multiple are running, `--session` to target a\nspecific session when multiple notebooks are open on the same server, or\n`--url` to skip discovery entirely and hit a server URL directly (e.g.\n`--url http://localhost:2718`). `--url` is the only way to connect to\nremote servers since auto-discovery only reads the local registry. **Only\nuse `--url` with trusted servers** — data is sent to the endpoint, so a\nmalicious URL could exfiltrate notebook contents. Set the `MARIMO_TOKEN`\nenv var to authenticate when the server has token auth enabled (`--token`\nflag also works but exposes the token in process listings). If the\nserver was started with `--mcp`, you'll have MCP tools available as an\nalternative.\n\n### Discovery finds nothing but the user has a server running?\n\nOnly `--no-token` servers are in the registry. If discovery comes up empty,\nthe server likely has token auth — ask the user for the token and set it as\nthe `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable.\n\n### No servers running?\n\n**Always discover before starting.** Background task \"completed\" notifications\ndo not mean the server died — check the output or run discover first.\n\nIf no servers are found, read the user's intent — if they want a notebook,\nstart one. **Always start marimo as a background task** (using\n`run_in_background` on the Bash tool) so the server automatically gets cleaned\nup when the session ends and doesn't block the conversation. See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md).\n\nIf there's no `.py` file yet, pick a descriptive filename based on context\n(e.g., `exploration.py`, `analysis.py`, `dashboard.py`). Don't ask — just\npick something reasonable.\n\n**Avoid shell escaping issues.** `-c` works for simple one-liners, but for\nmultiline code or code with quotes/backticks/`${}`, use a heredoc or a file:\n\n```bash\n# heredoc (single-quoted delimiter prevents shell interpolation)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\nEOF\n\n# file\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh /tmp/code.py\n\n# direct URL (skips auto-discovery and works with remote servers)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh --url http://localhost:2718 -c \"1 + 1\"\n```\n\n## Executing Code\n\nEvery execute-code call runs inside the notebook's kernel. All cell variables\nare in scope — `print(df.head())` just works. Nothing you define persists\nbetween calls (variables, imports, side-effects all reset), but you can freely\nintrospect the notebook: inspect variables, test code snippets, check types\nand shapes. Use this to explore, prototype, and validate before committing\nanything to the notebook — then create cells to persist state and make results\nvisible to the user.\n\nTo mutate the notebook's dataflow graph — create, edit, and delete cells,\ninstall packages, and run cells — use `marimo._code_mode`:\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    cid = ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\n    ctx.install_packages(\"pandas\")\n    ctx.run_cell(cid)\n```\n\nYou **must** use `async with` — without it, operations silently do nothing.\nAll `ctx.*` methods are **synchronous** — they queue operations and the\ncontext manager flushes them on exit. Do **not** `await` them.\n\n**Cells are not auto-executed.** `create_cell` and `edit_cell` are structural\nchanges only — use `run_cell` to queue execution.\n\n`code_mode` is a tested, safe API for notebook mutations — prefer it for all\nstructural changes. You also have access to marimo internals from the kernel,\nbut treat that as a last resort and only with high confidence after exploration.\n\n**UI state lives outside the reactive graph.** Anywidget traitlets can be read\nor set directly (e.g., `slider.value = 5`). For `mo.ui.*` elements, use\n`ctx.set_ui_value(element, new_value)` inside `code_mode`.\n\n### First Step: Explore the API\n\nThe `code_mode` API can change between marimo versions — and each running\nserver could be a different version. Inspect what's available at the start of\neach session, especially when switching between servers.\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx  # inspect me — dir(), help(), .cells, ...\n```\n\n## Guard Rails\n\nSkip these and the UI breaks:\n\n- **Install packages via `ctx.install_packages()`, not `uv add` or `pip`.**\n  The code API handles kernel restarts and dependency resolution correctly.\n  Only fall back to external CLIs if the API is unavailable or fails.\n- **Custom widget = anywidget.** For bespoke visual components, use anywidget\n  with HTML/CSS/JS. Composed `mo.ui` is fine for simple forms and controls.\n  See [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md).\n- **NEVER write to the `.py` file directly while a session is running — the kernel owns it.**\n- **No temp-file deps in cells.** `pathlib.Path(\"/tmp/...\")` in cell code is a bug.\n- **Avoid empty cells.** Prefer `edit_cell` into existing empty cells rather\n  than creating new ones. Clean up any cells that end up empty after edits.\n- **Don't worry about cell names.** Most cells don't need explicit names —\n  see [notebook-improvements.md](reference/notebook-improvements.md#cell-names).\n\n## Widgets and Reactivity\n\nAnywidget state (traitlets) lives outside marimo's reactive graph. To hook a\nwidget trait into the graph, pick one strategy per widget — never mix them:\n\n- **`mo.state` + `.observe()`** — you pick specific traits to bridge. Default choice.\n- **`mo.ui.anywidget()`** — wraps all synced traits into one reactive `.value`. Convenient but coarser.\n\nRead [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md) before wiring either.\n\n## Keep in Mind\n\n- **The user is editing too.** The notebook can change between your calls —\n  re-inspect notebook state if it's been a while since you last looked.\n- **Deletions are destructive.** Deleting a cell removes its variables from\n  kernel memory — restoring means recreating the cell and re-running it and\n  its dependents. If intent seems ambiguous, ask first.\n- **Installing packages changes the project.** `ctx.install_packages()` adds\n  real dependencies — confirm when it's not obvious from context.\n\n## {"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1775620242523,"sessionID":"ses_294c9a442ffe2u1rupbrxwDiTe","part":{"id":"prt_d6b36bc53001rehXBlVC7Et10K","messageID":"msg_d6b36a162001yXsp65dg4aCHXD","sessionID":"ses_294c9a442ffe2u1rupbrxwDiTe","type":"tool","tool":"read","callID":"call_10d8496172384f0c968a9b24","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"filePath":"/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/glm-analysis.py"},"output":"<path>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/glm-analysis.py</path>\n<type>file</type>\n<content>\n1: # /// script\n2: # requires-python = \">=3.13\"\n3: # dependencies = [\n4: #     \"altair==6.0.0\",\n5: #     \"marimo>=0.22.5\",\n6: #     \"numpy==2.4.4\",\n7: #     \"pandas==3.0.2\",\n8: # ]\n9: # ///\n10: \n11: import marimo\n12: \n13: __generated_with = \"0.22.5\"\n14: app = marimo.App()\n15: \n16: \n17: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n18: def _(mo):\n19:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n20:     ## Filter single point mutations\\n\\nThe mutation column contains both single point mutations (e.g., A111C) and multi-point mutations separated by semicolons. We filter for single point mutations only, then parse each mutation into its wildtype residue, position, and mutant residue.\n21:     \"\"\")\n22:     return\n23: \n24: \n25: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n26: def _(df_raw, pd):\n27:     import re\n28: \n29:     mutation_pattern = re.compile(r'^([A-Z])(\\d+)([A-Z])$')\n30: \n31:     def parse_mutation(mutation_str):\n32:         if not isinstance(mutation_str, str):\n33:             return None, None, None\n34:         match = mutation_pattern.match(mutation_str)\n35:         if match:\n36:             return match.group(1), int(match.group(2)), match.group(3)\n37:         return None, None, None\n38: \n39:     df_single = df_raw[~df_raw['mutation'].str.contains(';', na=False)].copy()\n40:     df_single[['wildtype', 'position', 'mutant']] = df_single['mutation'].apply(\n41:         lambda m: pd.Series(parse_mutation(m))\n42:     )\n43:     df_single = df_single.dropna(subset=['position'])\n44:     df_single['position'] = df_single['position'].astype(int)\n45:     df_single\n46:     return (df_single,)\n47: \n48: \n49: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n50: def _(mo):\n51:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n52:     ## Heatmap of single point mutations\\n\\nWe construct a heatmap with position on the x-axis, mutant residue on the y-axis, and the mean activity value as the color. This gives us an at-a-glance view of which positions and substitutions yield the highest activity.\n53:     \"\"\")\n54:     return\n55: \n56: \n57: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n58: def _(alt, df_single):\n59:     heatmap_data = df_single[['position', 'mutant', 'mean']].copy()\n60:     heatmap_chart = alt.Chart(heatmap_data).mark_rect().encode(\n61:         x=alt.X('position:O', title='Position'),\n62:         y=alt.Y('mutant:O', title='Mutant Residue', sort=alt.SortField('mutant')), \n63:         color=alt.Color('mean:Q', title='Mean Activity', scale=alt.Scale(scheme='viridis')),\n64:         tooltip=['position:O', 'mutant:O', 'mean:Q']\n65:     ).properties(\n66:         width=1200,\n67:         height=400,\n68:         title='Single Point Mutation Activity Heatmap'\n69:     )\n70:     heatmap_chart\n71:     return\n72: \n73: \n74: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n75: def _(mo):\n76:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n77:     ## Rank order by average mean\\n\\nWe group by position and compute the average mean activity across all mutant residues at each position. Positions are sorted from highest to lowest average, revealing which positions are most consistently active regardless of the specific substitution.\n78:     \"\"\")\n79:     return\n80: \n81: \n82: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n83: def _(df_single):\n84:     pos_avg_mean = df_single.groupby('position')['mean'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).reset_index()\n85:     pos_avg_mean.columns = ['position', 'avg_mean']\n86:     pos_avg_mean\n87:     return\n88: \n89: \n90: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n91: def _(mo):\n92:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n93:     ## Rank order by top mean\\n\\nWe group by position and take the maximum mean activity observed at each position. This highlights positions where at least one substitution achieves very high activity, even if other substitutions at that position are poor.\n94:     \"\"\")\n95:     return\n96: \n97: \n98: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n99: def _(df_single):\n100:     pos_top_mean = df_single.groupby('position')['mean'].max().sort_values(ascending=False).reset_index()\n101:     pos_top_mean.columns = ['position', 'top_mean']\n102:     pos_top_mean\n103:     return\n104: \n105: \n106: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n107: def _(mo):\n108:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n109:     ## Recommendation\\n\\nWe identify the top positions that appear in both the average-mean and top-mean rankings, since those positions are both consistently active and capable of reaching very high activity with the right substitution. Positions 243, 156, 212, 159, 242, 6, 247, 218, and 154 appear in the top 15 of both rankings, making them the strongest candidates for targeted mutagenesis. Among these, position 243 stands out as the top-ranked by average mean (0.294) and also has a top mean of 0.501, making it the single best position to mutate. {"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1775620228868,"sessionID":"ses_294c9a4d9ffeSwOQKf2XzFA0yJ","part":{"id":"prt_d6b3686f1001W5kMNaLqS2z4bQ","messageID":"msg_d6b365cfb001gz1CSS3Ztw38VH","sessionID":"ses_294c9a4d9ffeSwOQKf2XzFA0yJ","type":"tool","tool":"skill","callID":"functions.skill:0","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"name":"marimo-pair"},"output":"<skill_content name=\"marimo-pair\">\n# Skill: marimo-pair\n\n# marimo Pair Programming Protocol\n\nThis skill gives you full access to a running marimo notebook. You can read\ncell code, create and edit cells, install packages, run cells, and inspect\nthe reactive graph — all programmatically. The user sees results live in their\nbrowser while you work through bundled scripts or MCP.\n\n## Philosophy\n\nmarimo notebooks are a dataflow graph — cells are the fundamental unit of\ncomputation, connected by the variables they define and reference. When a cell\nruns, marimo automatically re-executes downstream cells. You have full access\nto the running notebook.\n\n- **Cells are your main lever.** Use them to break up work and choose how and\n  when to bring the human into the loop. Not every cell needs rich output —\n  sometimes the object itself is enough, sometimes a summary is better.\n  Match the presentation to the intent.\n- **Understand intent first.** When clear, act. When ambiguous, clarify.\n- **Follow existing signal.** Check imports, `pyproject.toml`, existing cells,\n  and `dir(ctx)` before reaching for external tools.\n- **Stay focused.** Build first, polish later — cell names, layout, and styling\n  can wait.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n### How to invoke marimo\n\nOnly servers started with `--no-token` register in the local server registry\nand are auto-discoverable — starting without a token makes discovery easier.\nIf a server has a token, set the `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable before\ncalling the execute script (avoids leaking the token in process listings). The\nright way to invoke marimo depends on context (project\ntooling, global install, sandbox mode). See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md) for the full decision tree.\n\n**Do NOT use `--headless` unless the user asks for it.** Omitting it lets\nmarimo auto-open the browser, which is the expected pairing experience. If the\nuser explicitly requests headless, offer to open it with\n`open http://localhost:<port>`.\n\n## How to Discover Servers and Execute Code\n\nTwo operations: **discover servers** and **execute code**.\n\n| Operation | Script | MCP |\n|-----------|--------|-----|\n| Discover servers | `bash scripts/discover-servers.sh` | `list_sessions()` tool |\n| Execute code | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh -c \"code\"` | `execute_code(code=..., session_id=...)` tool |\n| Execute code (multiline) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'` | same |\n| Execute code (direct URL) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh --url URL -c \"code\"` | same (with `url` param) |\n\nScripts auto-discover sessions from the registry on disk. Use `--port` to\ntarget a specific server when multiple are running, `--session` to target a\nspecific session when multiple notebooks are open on the same server, or\n`--url` to skip discovery entirely and hit a server URL directly (e.g.\n`--url http://localhost:2718`). `--url` is the only way to connect to\nremote servers since auto-discovery only reads the local registry. **Only\nuse `--url` with trusted servers** — data is sent to the endpoint, so a\nmalicious URL could exfiltrate notebook contents. Set the `MARIMO_TOKEN`\nenv var to authenticate when the server has token auth enabled (`--token`\nflag also works but exposes the token in process listings). If the\nserver was started with `--mcp`, you'll have MCP tools available as an\nalternative.\n\n### Discovery finds nothing but the user has a server running?\n\nOnly `--no-token` servers are in the registry. If discovery comes up empty,\nthe server likely has token auth — ask the user for the token and set it as\nthe `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable.\n\n### No servers running?\n\n**Always discover before starting.** Background task \"completed\" notifications\ndo not mean the server died — check the output or run discover first.\n\nIf no servers are found, read the user's intent — if they want a notebook,\nstart one. **Always start marimo as a background task** (using\n`run_in_background` on the Bash tool) so the server automatically gets cleaned\nup when the session ends and doesn't block the conversation. See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md).\n\nIf there's no `.py` file yet, pick a descriptive filename based on context\n(e.g., `exploration.py`, `analysis.py`, `dashboard.py`). Don't ask — just\npick something reasonable.\n\n**Avoid shell escaping issues.** `-c` works for simple one-liners, but for\nmultiline code or code with quotes/backticks/`${}`, use a heredoc or a file:\n\n```bash\n# heredoc (single-quoted delimiter prevents shell interpolation)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\nEOF\n\n# file\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh /tmp/code.py\n\n# direct URL (skips auto-discovery and works with remote servers)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh --url http://localhost:2718 -c \"1 + 1\"\n```\n\n## Executing Code\n\nEvery execute-code call runs inside the notebook's kernel. All cell variables\nare in scope — `print(df.head())` just works. Nothing you define persists\nbetween calls (variables, imports, side-effects all reset), but you can freely\nintrospect the notebook: inspect variables, test code snippets, check types\nand shapes. Use this to explore, prototype, and validate before committing\nanything to the notebook — then create cells to persist state and make results\nvisible to the user.\n\nTo mutate the notebook's dataflow graph — create, edit, and delete cells,\ninstall packages, and run cells — use `marimo._code_mode`:\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    cid = ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\n    ctx.install_packages(\"pandas\")\n    ctx.run_cell(cid)\n```\n\nYou **must** use `async with` — without it, operations silently do nothing.\nAll `ctx.*` methods are **synchronous** — they queue operations and the\ncontext manager flushes them on exit. Do **not** `await` them.\n\n**Cells are not auto-executed.** `create_cell` and `edit_cell` are structural\nchanges only — use `run_cell` to queue execution.\n\n`code_mode` is a tested, safe API for notebook mutations — prefer it for all\nstructural changes. You also have access to marimo internals from the kernel,\nbut treat that as a last resort and only with high confidence after exploration.\n\n**UI state lives outside the reactive graph.** Anywidget traitlets can be read\nor set directly (e.g., `slider.value = 5`). For `mo.ui.*` elements, use\n`ctx.set_ui_value(element, new_value)` inside `code_mode`.\n\n### First Step: Explore the API\n\nThe `code_mode` API can change between marimo versions — and each running\nserver could be a different version. Inspect what's available at the start of\neach session, especially when switching between servers.\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx  # inspect me — dir(), help(), .cells, ...\n```\n\n## Guard Rails\n\nSkip these and the UI breaks:\n\n- **Install packages via `ctx.install_packages()`, not `uv add` or `pip`.**\n  The code API handles kernel restarts and dependency resolution correctly.\n  Only fall back to external CLIs if the API is unavailable or fails.\n- **Custom widget = anywidget.** For bespoke visual components, use anywidget\n  with HTML/CSS/JS. Composed `mo.ui` is fine for simple forms and controls.\n  See [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md).\n- **NEVER write to the `.py` file directly while a session is running — the kernel owns it.**\n- **No temp-file deps in cells.** `pathlib.Path(\"/tmp/...\")` in cell code is a bug.\n- **Avoid empty cells.** Prefer `edit_cell` into existing empty cells rather\n  than creating new ones. Clean up any cells that end up empty after edits.\n- **Don't worry about cell names.** Most cells don't need explicit names —\n  see [notebook-improvements.md](reference/notebook-improvements.md#cell-names).\n\n## Widgets and Reactivity\n\nAnywidget state (traitlets) lives outside marimo's reactive graph. To hook a\nwidget trait into the graph, pick one strategy per widget — never mix them:\n\n- **`mo.state` + `.observe()`** — you pick specific traits to bridge. Default choice.\n- **`mo.ui.anywidget()`** — wraps all synced traits into one reactive `.value`. Convenient but coarser.\n\nRead [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md) before wiring either.\n\n## Keep in Mind\n\n- **The user is editing too.** The notebook can change between your calls —\n  re-inspect notebook state if it's been a while since you last looked.\n- **Deletions are destructive.** Deleting a cell removes its variables from\n  kernel memory — restoring means recreating the cell and re-running it and\n  its dependents. If intent seems ambiguous, ask first.\n- **Installing packages changes the project.** `ctx.install_packages()` adds\n  real dependencies — confirm when it's not obvious from context.\n\n## {"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1775620243525,"sessionID":"ses_294c9a4d9ffeSwOQKf2XzFA0yJ","part":{"id":"prt_d6b36bacc001ularkU0HK67YsT","messageID":"msg_d6b3697da001v0Hqa17OKBLmV9","sessionID":"ses_294c9a4d9ffeSwOQKf2XzFA0yJ","type":"tool","tool":"read","callID":"read:3","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"filePath":"/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/kimi-analysis.py"},"output":"<path>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/kimi-analysis.py</path>\n<type>file</type>\n<content>\n1: # /// script\n2: # requires-python = \">=3.13\"\n3: # dependencies = [\n4: #     \"marimo>=0.22.5\",\n5: #     \"matplotlib==3.10.8\",\n6: #     \"pandas==3.0.2\",\n7: #     \"seaborn==0.13.2\",\n8: # ]\n9: # ///\n10: \n11: import marimo\n12: \n13: __generated_with = \"0.22.5\"\n14: app = marimo.App()\n15: \n16: \n17: @app.cell\n18: def _():\n19:     return\n20: \n21: \n22: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n23: def _(mo):\n24:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n25:     # Single Point Mutation Analysis\n26: \n27:     This notebook analyzes single point mutations from the IRED Novartis dataset.\n28:     We visualize mutation effects as a heatmap and rank positions by their impact.\n29:     \"\"\")\n30:     return\n31: \n32: \n33: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n34: def _(mo):\n35:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n36:     ## Imports\n37: \n38:     We import the necessary libraries for data manipulation and visualization.\n39:     \"\"\")\n40:     return\n41: \n42: \n43: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n44: def _():\n45:     import pandas as pd\n46:     import marimo as mo\n47:     import seaborn as sns\n48:     import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n49:     from pathlib import Path\n50: \n51:     return Path, mo, pd, plt, sns\n52: \n53: \n54: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n55: def _(mo):\n56:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n57:     ## Data Loading\n58: \n59:     We load the mutation data from the CSV file. Each row contains a mutation\n60:     in the format `[OriginalAA][Position][MutantAA]` (e.g., A111C), along with\n61:     activity measurements including the 'mean' column that we will use for our analysis.\n62:     \"\"\")\n63:     return\n64: \n65: \n66: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n67: def _(Path, pd):\n68:     data_path = Path(\"data/ired-novartis/cs1c02786_si_002.csv\")\n69:     df_raw = pd.read_csv(data_path)\n70:     df_raw.head()\n71:     return (df_raw,)\n72: \n73: \n74: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n75: def _(mo):\n76:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n77:     ## Parse Mutation Information\n78: \n79:     We extract the position number and mutant amino acid letter from each mutation string.\n80:     The format is `[OriginalAA][Position][MutantAA]` - we need the position for the x-axis\n81:     and the mutant letter for the y-axis of our heatmap.\n82:     \"\"\")\n83:     return\n84: \n85: \n86: @app.cell(hide_code=True)\n87: def _(mo):\n88:     mo.md(r\"\"\"\n89:     ## Mutation Heatmap\n90: \n91:     We create a heatmap showing the mean activity values for each position (x-axis)\n92:     and mutant amino acid (y-axis). {"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1775620227812,"sessionID":"ses_294c9a6e7ffeaOVIP2r2YXTgJy","part":{"id":"prt_d6b3682ca001XZC4x2KZZTo47I","messageID":"msg_d6b365aea0017fggtzqtN37pke","sessionID":"ses_294c9a6e7ffeaOVIP2r2YXTgJy","type":"tool","tool":"skill","callID":"call_function_087wca6umeg2_1","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"name":"marimo-pair"},"output":"<skill_content name=\"marimo-pair\">\n# Skill: marimo-pair\n\n# marimo Pair Programming Protocol\n\nThis skill gives you full access to a running marimo notebook. You can read\ncell code, create and edit cells, install packages, run cells, and inspect\nthe reactive graph — all programmatically. The user sees results live in their\nbrowser while you work through bundled scripts or MCP.\n\n## Philosophy\n\nmarimo notebooks are a dataflow graph — cells are the fundamental unit of\ncomputation, connected by the variables they define and reference. When a cell\nruns, marimo automatically re-executes downstream cells. You have full access\nto the running notebook.\n\n- **Cells are your main lever.** Use them to break up work and choose how and\n  when to bring the human into the loop. Not every cell needs rich output —\n  sometimes the object itself is enough, sometimes a summary is better.\n  Match the presentation to the intent.\n- **Understand intent first.** When clear, act. When ambiguous, clarify.\n- **Follow existing signal.** Check imports, `pyproject.toml`, existing cells,\n  and `dir(ctx)` before reaching for external tools.\n- **Stay focused.** Build first, polish later — cell names, layout, and styling\n  can wait.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n### How to invoke marimo\n\nOnly servers started with `--no-token` register in the local server registry\nand are auto-discoverable — starting without a token makes discovery easier.\nIf a server has a token, set the `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable before\ncalling the execute script (avoids leaking the token in process listings). The\nright way to invoke marimo depends on context (project\ntooling, global install, sandbox mode). See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md) for the full decision tree.\n\n**Do NOT use `--headless` unless the user asks for it.** Omitting it lets\nmarimo auto-open the browser, which is the expected pairing experience. If the\nuser explicitly requests headless, offer to open it with\n`open http://localhost:<port>`.\n\n## How to Discover Servers and Execute Code\n\nTwo operations: **discover servers** and **execute code**.\n\n| Operation | Script | MCP |\n|-----------|--------|-----|\n| Discover servers | `bash scripts/discover-servers.sh` | `list_sessions()` tool |\n| Execute code | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh -c \"code\"` | `execute_code(code=..., session_id=...)` tool |\n| Execute code (multiline) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'` | same |\n| Execute code (direct URL) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh --url URL -c \"code\"` | same (with `url` param) |\n\nScripts auto-discover sessions from the registry on disk. Use `--port` to\ntarget a specific server when multiple are running, `--session` to target a\nspecific session when multiple notebooks are open on the same server, or\n`--url` to skip discovery entirely and hit a server URL directly (e.g.\n`--url http://localhost:2718`). `--url` is the only way to connect to\nremote servers since auto-discovery only reads the local registry. **Only\nuse `--url` with trusted servers** — data is sent to the endpoint, so a\nmalicious URL could exfiltrate notebook contents. Set the `MARIMO_TOKEN`\nenv var to authenticate when the server has token auth enabled (`--token`\nflag also works but exposes the token in process listings). If the\nserver was started with `--mcp`, you'll have MCP tools available as an\nalternative.\n\n### Discovery finds nothing but the user has a server running?\n\nOnly `--no-token` servers are in the registry. If discovery comes up empty,\nthe server likely has token auth — ask the user for the token and set it as\nthe `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable.\n\n### No servers running?\n\n**Always discover before starting.** Background task \"completed\" notifications\ndo not mean the server died — check the output or run discover first.\n\nIf no servers are found, read the user's intent — if they want a notebook,\nstart one. **Always start marimo as a background task** (using\n`run_in_background` on the Bash tool) so the server automatically gets cleaned\nup when the session ends and doesn't block the conversation. See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md).\n\nIf there's no `.py` file yet, pick a descriptive filename based on context\n(e.g., `exploration.py`, `analysis.py`, `dashboard.py`). Don't ask — just\npick something reasonable.\n\n**Avoid shell escaping issues.** `-c` works for simple one-liners, but for\nmultiline code or code with quotes/backticks/`${}`, use a heredoc or a file:\n\n```bash\n# heredoc (single-quoted delimiter prevents shell interpolation)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\nEOF\n\n# file\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh /tmp/code.py\n\n# direct URL (skips auto-discovery and works with remote servers)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh --url http://localhost:2718 -c \"1 + 1\"\n```\n\n## Executing Code\n\nEvery execute-code call runs inside the notebook's kernel. All cell variables\nare in scope — `print(df.head())` just works. Nothing you define persists\nbetween calls (variables, imports, side-effects all reset), but you can freely\nintrospect the notebook: inspect variables, test code snippets, check types\nand shapes. Use this to explore, prototype, and validate before committing\nanything to the notebook — then create cells to persist state and make results\nvisible to the user.\n\nTo mutate the notebook's dataflow graph — create, edit, and delete cells,\ninstall packages, and run cells — use `marimo._code_mode`:\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    cid = ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\n    ctx.install_packages(\"pandas\")\n    ctx.run_cell(cid)\n```\n\nYou **must** use `async with` — without it, operations silently do nothing.\nAll `ctx.*` methods are **synchronous** — they queue operations and the\ncontext manager flushes them on exit. Do **not** `await` them.\n\n**Cells are not auto-executed.** `create_cell` and `edit_cell` are structural\nchanges only — use `run_cell` to queue execution.\n\n`code_mode` is a tested, safe API for notebook mutations — prefer it for all\nstructural changes. You also have access to marimo internals from the kernel,\nbut treat that as a last resort and only with high confidence after exploration.\n\n**UI state lives outside the reactive graph.** Anywidget traitlets can be read\nor set directly (e.g., `slider.value = 5`). For `mo.ui.*` elements, use\n`ctx.set_ui_value(element, new_value)` inside `code_mode`.\n\n### First Step: Explore the API\n\nThe `code_mode` API can change between marimo versions — and each running\nserver could be a different version. Inspect what's available at the start of\neach session, especially when switching between servers.\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx  # inspect me — dir(), help(), .cells, ...\n```\n\n## Guard Rails\n\nSkip these and the UI breaks:\n\n- **Install packages via `ctx.install_packages()`, not `uv add` or `pip`.**\n  The code API handles kernel restarts and dependency resolution correctly.\n  Only fall back to external CLIs if the API is unavailable or fails.\n- **Custom widget = anywidget.** For bespoke visual components, use anywidget\n  with HTML/CSS/JS. Composed `mo.ui` is fine for simple forms and controls.\n  See [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md).\n- **NEVER write to the `.py` file directly while a session is running — the kernel owns it.**\n- **No temp-file deps in cells.** `pathlib.Path(\"/tmp/...\")` in cell code is a bug.\n- **Avoid empty cells.** Prefer `edit_cell` into existing empty cells rather\n  than creating new ones. Clean up any cells that end up empty after edits.\n- **Don't worry about cell names.** Most cells don't need explicit names —\n  see [notebook-improvements.md](reference/notebook-improvements.md#cell-names).\n\n## Widgets and Reactivity\n\nAnywidget state (traitlets) lives outside marimo's reactive graph. To hook a\nwidget trait into the graph, pick one strategy per widget — never mix them:\n\n- **`mo.state` + `.observe()`** — you pick specific traits to bridge. Default choice.\n- **`mo.ui.anywidget()`** — wraps all synced traits into one reactive `.value`. Convenient but coarser.\n\nRead [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md) before wiring either.\n\n## Keep in Mind\n\n- **The user is editing too.** The notebook can change between your calls —\n  re-inspect notebook state if it's been a while since you last looked.\n- **Deletions are destructive.** Deleting a cell removes its variables from\n  kernel memory — restoring means recreating the cell and re-running it and\n  its dependents. If intent seems ambiguous, ask first.\n- **Installing packages changes the project.** `ctx.install_packages()` adds\n  real dependencies — confirm when it's not obvious from context.\n\n## References\n\n- [finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md) — how to find and invoke the right marimo\n- [gotchas.md](reference/gotchas.md) — cached module proxies and other traps\n- [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md) — custom widgets and visualizations\n- [notebook-improvements.md](reference/notebook-improvements.md) — improving existing {"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1775620223166,"sessionID":"ses_294c9a59bffeejOnw7iheHHCCR","part":{"id":"prt_d6b3670a10014hDlKezGGbFpNw","messageID":"msg_d6b365c43001cn8uWFgV8kecwm","sessionID":"ses_294c9a59bffeejOnw7iheHHCCR","type":"tool","tool":"skill","callID":"toolu_bdrk_01JqyEutadxG31JLfhHD9CxA","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"name":"marimo-pair"},"output":"<skill_content name=\"marimo-pair\">\n# Skill: marimo-pair\n\n# marimo Pair Programming Protocol\n\nThis skill gives you full access to a running marimo notebook. You can read\ncell code, create and edit cells, install packages, run cells, and inspect\nthe reactive graph — all programmatically. The user sees results live in their\nbrowser while you work through bundled scripts or MCP.\n\n## Philosophy\n\nmarimo notebooks are a dataflow graph — cells are the fundamental unit of\ncomputation, connected by the variables they define and reference. When a cell\nruns, marimo automatically re-executes downstream cells. You have full access\nto the running notebook.\n\n- **Cells are your main lever.** Use them to break up work and choose how and\n  when to bring the human into the loop. Not every cell needs rich output —\n  sometimes the object itself is enough, sometimes a summary is better.\n  Match the presentation to the intent.\n- **Understand intent first.** When clear, act. When ambiguous, clarify.\n- **Follow existing signal.** Check imports, `pyproject.toml`, existing cells,\n  and `dir(ctx)` before reaching for external tools.\n- **Stay focused.** Build first, polish later — cell names, layout, and styling\n  can wait.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n### How to invoke marimo\n\nOnly servers started with `--no-token` register in the local server registry\nand are auto-discoverable — starting without a token makes discovery easier.\nIf a server has a token, set the `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable before\ncalling the execute script (avoids leaking the token in process listings). The\nright way to invoke marimo depends on context (project\ntooling, global install, sandbox mode). See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md) for the full decision tree.\n\n**Do NOT use `--headless` unless the user asks for it.** Omitting it lets\nmarimo auto-open the browser, which is the expected pairing experience. If the\nuser explicitly requests headless, offer to open it with\n`open http://localhost:<port>`.\n\n## How to Discover Servers and Execute Code\n\nTwo operations: **discover servers** and **execute code**.\n\n| Operation | Script | MCP |\n|-----------|--------|-----|\n| Discover servers | `bash scripts/discover-servers.sh` | `list_sessions()` tool |\n| Execute code | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh -c \"code\"` | `execute_code(code=..., session_id=...)` tool |\n| Execute code (multiline) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'` | same |\n| Execute code (direct URL) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh --url URL -c \"code\"` | same (with `url` param) |\n\nScripts auto-discover sessions from the registry on disk. Use `--port` to\ntarget a specific server when multiple are running, `--session` to target a\nspecific session when multiple notebooks are open on the same server, or\n`--url` to skip discovery entirely and hit a server URL directly (e.g.\n`--url http://localhost:2718`). `--url` is the only way to connect to\nremote servers since auto-discovery only reads the local registry. **Only\nuse `--url` with trusted servers** — data is sent to the endpoint, so a\nmalicious URL could exfiltrate notebook contents. Set the `MARIMO_TOKEN`\nenv var to authenticate when the server has token auth enabled (`--token`\nflag also works but exposes the token in process listings). If the\nserver was started with `--mcp`, you'll have MCP tools available as an\nalternative.\n\n### Discovery finds nothing but the user has a server running?\n\nOnly `--no-token` servers are in the registry. If discovery comes up empty,\nthe server likely has token auth — ask the user for the token and set it as\nthe `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable.\n\n### No servers running?\n\n**Always discover before starting.** Background task \"completed\" notifications\ndo not mean the server died — check the output or run discover first.\n\nIf no servers are found, read the user's intent — if they want a notebook,\nstart one. **Always start marimo as a background task** (using\n`run_in_background` on the Bash tool) so the server automatically gets cleaned\nup when the session ends and doesn't block the conversation. See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md).\n\nIf there's no `.py` file yet, pick a descriptive filename based on context\n(e.g., `exploration.py`, `analysis.py`, `dashboard.py`). Don't ask — just\npick something reasonable.\n\n**Avoid shell escaping issues.** `-c` works for simple one-liners, but for\nmultiline code or code with quotes/backticks/`${}`, use a heredoc or a file:\n\n```bash\n# heredoc (single-quoted delimiter prevents shell interpolation)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\nEOF\n\n# file\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh /tmp/code.py\n\n# direct URL (skips auto-discovery and works with remote servers)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh --url http://localhost:2718 -c \"1 + 1\"\n```\n\n## Executing Code\n\nEvery execute-code call runs inside the notebook's kernel. All cell variables\nare in scope — `print(df.head())` just works. Nothing you define persists\nbetween calls (variables, imports, side-effects all reset), but you can freely\nintrospect the notebook: inspect variables, test code snippets, check types\nand shapes. Use this to explore, prototype, and validate before committing\nanything to the notebook — then create cells to persist state and make results\nvisible to the user.\n\nTo mutate the notebook's dataflow graph — create, edit, and delete cells,\ninstall packages, and run cells — use `marimo._code_mode`:\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    cid = ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\n    ctx.install_packages(\"pandas\")\n    ctx.run_cell(cid)\n```\n\nYou **must** use `async with` — without it, operations silently do nothing.\nAll `ctx.*` methods are **synchronous** — they queue operations and the\ncontext manager flushes them on exit. Do **not** `await` them.\n\n**Cells are not auto-executed.** `create_cell` and `edit_cell` are structural\nchanges only — use `run_cell` to queue execution.\n\n`code_mode` is a tested, safe API for notebook mutations — prefer it for all\nstructural changes. You also have access to marimo internals from the kernel,\nbut treat that as a last resort and only with high confidence after exploration.\n\n**UI state lives outside the reactive graph.** Anywidget traitlets can be read\nor set directly (e.g., `slider.value = 5`). For `mo.ui.*` elements, use\n`ctx.set_ui_value(element, new_value)` inside `code_mode`.\n\n### First Step: Explore the API\n\nThe `code_mode` API can change between marimo versions — and each running\nserver could be a different version. Inspect what's available at the start of\neach session, especially when switching between servers.\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx  # inspect me — dir(), help(), .cells, ...\n```\n\n## Guard Rails\n\nSkip these and the UI breaks:\n\n- **Install packages via `ctx.install_packages()`, not `uv add` or `pip`.**\n  The code API handles kernel restarts and dependency resolution correctly.\n  Only fall back to external CLIs if the API is unavailable or fails.\n- **Custom widget = anywidget.** For bespoke visual components, use anywidget\n  with HTML/CSS/JS. Composed `mo.ui` is fine for simple forms and controls.\n  See [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md).\n- **NEVER write to the `.py` file directly while a session is running — the kernel owns it.**\n- **No temp-file deps in cells.** `pathlib.Path(\"/tmp/...\")` in cell code is a bug.\n- **Avoid empty cells.** Prefer `edit_cell` into existing empty cells rather\n  than creating new ones. Clean up any cells that end up empty after edits.\n- **Don't worry about cell names.** Most cells don't need explicit names —\n  see [notebook-improvements.md](reference/notebook-improvements.md#cell-names).\n\n## Widgets and Reactivity\n\nAnywidget state (traitlets) lives outside marimo's reactive graph. To hook a\nwidget trait into the graph, pick one strategy per widget — never mix them:\n\n- **`mo.state` + `.observe()`** — you pick specific traits to bridge. Default choice.\n- **`mo.ui.anywidget()`** — wraps all synced traits into one reactive `.value`. Convenient but coarser.\n\nRead [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md) before wiring either.\n\n## Keep in Mind\n\n- **The user is editing too.** The notebook can change between your calls —\n  re-inspect notebook state if it's been a while since you last looked.\n- **Deletions are destructive.** Deleting a cell removes its variables from\n  kernel memory — restoring means recreating the cell and re-running it and\n  its dependents. If intent seems ambiguous, ask first.\n- **Installing packages changes the project.** `ctx.install_packages()` adds\n  real dependencies — confirm when it's not obvious from context.\n\n## References\n\n- [finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md) — how to find and invoke the right marimo\n- [gotchas.md](reference/gotchas.md) — cached module proxies and other traps\n- [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md) — custom widgets and visualizations\n- [notebook-improvements.md](reference/notebook-improvements.md) — improving existing notebooks\n\nBase directory for this skill: file:///Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair\nRelative paths in this skill (e.g., scripts/, reference/) are relative to this base directory.\nNote: file list is sampled.\n\n<skill_files>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/reference/finding-marimo.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/reference/notebook-improvements.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/reference/rich-representations.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/reference/gotchas.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/scripts/discover-servers.sh</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/scripts/execute-code.sh</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/README.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/LICENSE</file>\n</skill_files>\n</skill_content>","metadata":{"name":"marimo-pair","dir":"/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair","truncated":false},"title":"Loaded skill: marimo-pair","time":{"start":1775620223148,"end":1775620223165}},"metadata":{"openrouter":{"reasoning_details":[]}}}}

{"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1775620221047,"sessionID":"ses_294c9a7a5ffeyRhTzANIuGCQn6","part":{"id":"prt_d6b3668640016BFVVTUpdF4yW3","messageID":"msg_d6b365a1d001F9bMUuk88j9Lti","sessionID":"ses_294c9a7a5ffeyRhTzANIuGCQn6","type":"tool","tool":"skill","callID":"call_e8a550b94c4464c6","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"name":"marimo-pair"},"output":"<skill_content name=\"marimo-pair\">\n# Skill: marimo-pair\n\n# marimo Pair Programming Protocol\n\nThis skill gives you full access to a running marimo notebook. You can read\ncell code, create and edit cells, install packages, run cells, and inspect\nthe reactive graph — all programmatically. The user sees results live in their\nbrowser while you work through bundled scripts or MCP.\n\n## Philosophy\n\nmarimo notebooks are a dataflow graph — cells are the fundamental unit of\ncomputation, connected by the variables they define and reference. When a cell\nruns, marimo automatically re-executes downstream cells. You have full access\nto the running notebook.\n\n- **Cells are your main lever.** Use them to break up work and choose how and\n  when to bring the human into the loop. Not every cell needs rich output —\n  sometimes the object itself is enough, sometimes a summary is better.\n  Match the presentation to the intent.\n- **Understand intent first.** When clear, act. When ambiguous, clarify.\n- **Follow existing signal.** Check imports, `pyproject.toml`, existing cells,\n  and `dir(ctx)` before reaching for external tools.\n- **Stay focused.** Build first, polish later — cell names, layout, and styling\n  can wait.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n### How to invoke marimo\n\nOnly servers started with `--no-token` register in the local server registry\nand are auto-discoverable — starting without a token makes discovery easier.\nIf a server has a token, set the `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable before\ncalling the execute script (avoids leaking the token in process listings). The\nright way to invoke marimo depends on context (project\ntooling, global install, sandbox mode). See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md) for the full decision tree.\n\n**Do NOT use `--headless` unless the user asks for it.** Omitting it lets\nmarimo auto-open the browser, which is the expected pairing experience. If the\nuser explicitly requests headless, offer to open it with\n`open http://localhost:<port>`.\n\n## How to Discover Servers and Execute Code\n\nTwo operations: **discover servers** and **execute code**.\n\n| Operation | Script | MCP |\n|-----------|--------|-----|\n| Discover servers | `bash scripts/discover-servers.sh` | `list_sessions()` tool |\n| Execute code | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh -c \"code\"` | `execute_code(code=..., session_id=...)` tool |\n| Execute code (multiline) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'` | same |\n| Execute code (direct URL) | `bash scripts/execute-code.sh --url URL -c \"code\"` | same (with `url` param) |\n\nScripts auto-discover sessions from the registry on disk. Use `--port` to\ntarget a specific server when multiple are running, `--session` to target a\nspecific session when multiple notebooks are open on the same server, or\n`--url` to skip discovery entirely and hit a server URL directly (e.g.\n`--url http://localhost:2718`). `--url` is the only way to connect to\nremote servers since auto-discovery only reads the local registry. **Only\nuse `--url` with trusted servers** — data is sent to the endpoint, so a\nmalicious URL could exfiltrate notebook contents. Set the `MARIMO_TOKEN`\nenv var to authenticate when the server has token auth enabled (`--token`\nflag also works but exposes the token in process listings). If the\nserver was started with `--mcp`, you'll have MCP tools available as an\nalternative.\n\n### Discovery finds nothing but the user has a server running?\n\nOnly `--no-token` servers are in the registry. If discovery comes up empty,\nthe server likely has token auth — ask the user for the token and set it as\nthe `MARIMO_TOKEN` environment variable.\n\n### No servers running?\n\n**Always discover before starting.** Background task \"completed\" notifications\ndo not mean the server died — check the output or run discover first.\n\nIf no servers are found, read the user's intent — if they want a notebook,\nstart one. **Always start marimo as a background task** (using\n`run_in_background` on the Bash tool) so the server automatically gets cleaned\nup when the session ends and doesn't block the conversation. See\n[finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md).\n\nIf there's no `.py` file yet, pick a descriptive filename based on context\n(e.g., `exploration.py`, `analysis.py`, `dashboard.py`). Don't ask — just\npick something reasonable.\n\n**Avoid shell escaping issues.** `-c` works for simple one-liners, but for\nmultiline code or code with quotes/backticks/`${}`, use a heredoc or a file:\n\n```bash\n# heredoc (single-quoted delimiter prevents shell interpolation)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh <<'EOF'\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\nEOF\n\n# file\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh /tmp/code.py\n\n# direct URL (skips auto-discovery and works with remote servers)\nbash scripts/execute-code.sh --url http://localhost:2718 -c \"1 + 1\"\n```\n\n## Executing Code\n\nEvery execute-code call runs inside the notebook's kernel. All cell variables\nare in scope — `print(df.head())` just works. Nothing you define persists\nbetween calls (variables, imports, side-effects all reset), but you can freely\nintrospect the notebook: inspect variables, test code snippets, check types\nand shapes. Use this to explore, prototype, and validate before committing\nanything to the notebook — then create cells to persist state and make results\nvisible to the user.\n\nTo mutate the notebook's dataflow graph — create, edit, and delete cells,\ninstall packages, and run cells — use `marimo._code_mode`:\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    cid = ctx.create_cell(\"x = 1\")\n    ctx.install_packages(\"pandas\")\n    ctx.run_cell(cid)\n```\n\nYou **must** use `async with` — without it, operations silently do nothing.\nAll `ctx.*` methods are **synchronous** — they queue operations and the\ncontext manager flushes them on exit. Do **not** `await` them.\n\n**Cells are not auto-executed.** `create_cell` and `edit_cell` are structural\nchanges only — use `run_cell` to queue execution.\n\n`code_mode` is a tested, safe API for notebook mutations — prefer it for all\nstructural changes. You also have access to marimo internals from the kernel,\nbut treat that as a last resort and only with high confidence after exploration.\n\n**UI state lives outside the reactive graph.** Anywidget traitlets can be read\nor set directly (e.g., `slider.value = 5`). For `mo.ui.*` elements, use\n`ctx.set_ui_value(element, new_value)` inside `code_mode`.\n\n### First Step: Explore the API\n\nThe `code_mode` API can change between marimo versions — and each running\nserver could be a different version. Inspect what's available at the start of\neach session, especially when switching between servers.\n\n```python\nimport marimo._code_mode as cm\n\nasync with cm.get_context() as ctx:\n    ctx  # inspect me — dir(), help(), .cells, ...\n```\n\n## Guard Rails\n\nSkip these and the UI breaks:\n\n- **Install packages via `ctx.install_packages()`, not `uv add` or `pip`.**\n  The code API handles kernel restarts and dependency resolution correctly.\n  Only fall back to external CLIs if the API is unavailable or fails.\n- **Custom widget = anywidget.** For bespoke visual components, use anywidget\n  with HTML/CSS/JS. Composed `mo.ui` is fine for simple forms and controls.\n  See [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md).\n- **NEVER write to the `.py` file directly while a session is running — the kernel owns it.**\n- **No temp-file deps in cells.** `pathlib.Path(\"/tmp/...\")` in cell code is a bug.\n- **Avoid empty cells.** Prefer `edit_cell` into existing empty cells rather\n  than creating new ones. Clean up any cells that end up empty after edits.\n- **Don't worry about cell names.** Most cells don't need explicit names —\n  see [notebook-improvements.md](reference/notebook-improvements.md#cell-names).\n\n## Widgets and Reactivity\n\nAnywidget state (traitlets) lives outside marimo's reactive graph. To hook a\nwidget trait into the graph, pick one strategy per widget — never mix them:\n\n- **`mo.state` + `.observe()`** — you pick specific traits to bridge. Default choice.\n- **`mo.ui.anywidget()`** — wraps all synced traits into one reactive `.value`. Convenient but coarser.\n\nRead [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md) before wiring either.\n\n## Keep in Mind\n\n- **The user is editing too.** The notebook can change between your calls —\n  re-inspect notebook state if it's been a while since you last looked.\n- **Deletions are destructive.** Deleting a cell removes its variables from\n  kernel memory — restoring means recreating the cell and re-running it and\n  its dependents. If intent seems ambiguous, ask first.\n- **Installing packages changes the project.** `ctx.install_packages()` adds\n  real dependencies — confirm when it's not obvious from context.\n\n## References\n\n- [finding-marimo.md](reference/finding-marimo.md) — how to find and invoke the right marimo\n- [gotchas.md](reference/gotchas.md) — cached module proxies and other traps\n- [rich-representations.md](reference/rich-representations.md) — custom widgets and visualizations\n- [notebook-improvements.md](reference/notebook-improvements.md) — improving existing notebooks\n\nBase directory for this skill: file:///Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair\nRelative paths in this skill (e.g., scripts/, reference/) are relative to this base directory.\nNote: file list is sampled.\n\n<skill_files>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/reference/finding-marimo.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/reference/notebook-improvements.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/reference/rich-representations.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/reference/gotchas.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/scripts/discover-servers.sh</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/scripts/execute-code.sh</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/README.md</file>\n<file>/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair/LICENSE</file>\n</skill_files>\n</skill_content>","metadata":{"name":"marimo-pair","dir":"/Users/ericmjl/github/marimo-pair-benchmark/.agents/skills/marimo-pair","truncated":false},"title":"Loaded skill: marimo-pair","time":{"start":1775620221031,"end":1775620221046}},"metadata":{"openrouter":{"reasoning_details":[]}}}}
